agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A production logistics system enforces a strict 1:1 Shipment-to-Carrier invariant with supervisor-gated reassignment. The product team requests an auto-split feature that would allow shipments over 500 kg to be divided among multiple carriers without supervisor involvement, directly conflicting with two established domain rules.","bounded_context":"Shipment Management / Carrier Assignment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"A unit of freight to be transported, guaranteed to have exactly one assigned Carrier at dispatch time."},{"term":"Carrier","definition":"The party responsible for physically transporting a Shipment."},{"term":"Sub-shipment","definition":"A proposed new concept: a child Shipment created by splitting a parent Shipment that exceeds a weight threshold. Not yet part of the domain model."},{"term":"Auto-split","definition":"Proposed automatic decomposition of an overweight Shipment into multiple sub-shipments, each assigned to a different Carrier, bypassing Supervisor approval."},{"term":"Supervisor","definition":"A privileged role that is the sole authority permitted to reassign a Carrier on a Shipment."},{"term":"Carrier Assignment Lock","definition":"The invariant that once a Carrier is assigned to a Shipment, only a Supervisor can change or reassign it."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a data integrity constraint enforced at dispatch time. Violating it means a Shipment could be dispatched with zero or multiple Carriers, creating ambiguous responsibility and liability.","testable_assertion":"At any point in its lifecycle, a Shipment entity references exactly one Carrier."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Once a Carrier is assigned to a Shipment, only a Supervisor can reassign it.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is an access-control integrity constraint preventing unauthorized mutation of a critical assignment. Removing it undermines the chain-of-custody guarantee.","testable_assertion":"Any attempt to change the Carrier on a Shipment without Supervisor authorization is rejected by the system."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Auto-split proposes that shipments exceeding 500 kg are automatically divided into sub-shipments, each assigned to a different Carrier without Supervisor involvement.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The 500 kg threshold is a business policy that could be tuned. However, the mechanism itself conflicts with BR-1 and BR-2.","testable_assertion":"When a Shipment weight exceeds 500 kg, the system creates N sub-shipments each weighing <= 500 kg and assigns a distinct Carrier to each."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A Shipment with weight > 500 kg is created","when":"The auto-split feature triggers","then":"The original Shipment is decomposed into N sub-shipments, each with weight <= 500 kg, and each sub-shipment has exactly one assigned Carrier (preserving BR-1 per sub-shipment)."},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A Shipment has been auto-split into sub-shipments","when":"A non-Supervisor user attempts to reassign a Carrier on any sub-shipment","then":"The system rejects the reassignment (preserving BR-2 per sub-shipment)."},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A Shipment with weight <= 500 kg","when":"The Shipment proceeds through normal flow","then":"No splitting occurs; the existing 1:1 Carrier assignment model is unchanged."},{"id":"AC-4","given":"An auto-split is about to occur on a Shipment that already has a locked Carrier assignment","when":"The split would reassign or remove the original Carrier","then":"The system must resolve whether this constitutes a reassignment requiring Supervisor approval — this is the core contradiction."}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Shipment weighs exactly 500 kg","expected_behavior":"No split occurs — the threshold is 'exceeds 500 kg', so exactly 500 kg is handled normally."},{"scenario":"Shipment weighs 501 kg — split produces one sub-shipment of 500 kg and one of 1 kg","expected_behavior":"Both sub-shipments must each have a valid Carrier. The 1 kg sub-shipment still needs a full Carrier assignment, raising cost-efficiency questions."},{"scenario":"Original Shipment already has a locked Carrier assignment when auto-split triggers","expected_behavior":"Unclear: does the original Carrier get one sub-shipment? Are the new Carrier assignments considered reassignments requiring Supervisor approval? This is the heart of the contradiction."},{"scenario":"A sub-shipment fails delivery — who owns the parent Shipment's SLA?","expected_behavior":"Undefined. The domain model has no concept of partial delivery or parent-child Shipment relationships."},{"scenario":"Carrier assigned to original Shipment is not suitable for any sub-shipment (e.g., capacity constraints)","expected_behavior":"The locked assignment must be broken, which currently requires a Supervisor. Auto-split without Supervisor involvement violates BR-2."},{"scenario":"Recursive split: a sub-shipment's cargo is indivisible and still exceeds 500 kg","expected_behavior":"Undefined. The feature request does not address indivisible freight or recursive splitting."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Potential cost increase from assigning multiple Carriers to what was a single Shipment. Minimum-charge economics may make tiny sub-shipments (e.g., 1 kg remainder) disproportionately expensive.","operational":"Introduces a parent-child Shipment relationship that does not exist in the current model. Tracking, SLA management, invoicing, and exception handling all need redesign. Significant operational complexity increase.","user":"Supervisors lose visibility and control over Carrier assignments for heavy shipments. Customers may see a single order fragmented into multiple tracking numbers without understanding why.","risk":"HIGH. The feature as specified cannot be implemented without violating two production invariants. Shipping it as described risks data integrity issues, liability gaps, and regression in the existing assignment workflow.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Supervisors","impact":"Their exclusive authority over Carrier reassignment is bypassed for auto-split shipments, undermining their operational control role."},{"group":"Carriers","impact":"May receive sub-shipments they did not agree to. Contractual and capacity implications are unaddressed."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"A single shipment becomes multiple trackable units — potential confusion, partial delivery scenarios, and SLA ambiguity."},{"group":"Finance / Billing","impact":"Invoicing model assumes 1 Shipment = 1 Carrier. Multi-carrier sub-shipments require a new billing structure."},{"group":"Engineering","impact":"Must introduce Sub-shipment aggregate, parent-child relationships, and reconcile with the existing invariant-enforcing code without regression."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"The specification contains two direct contradictions with established production invariants. Until the product team clarifies whether the invariants should be relaxed, restructured, or preserved via a new aggregate design, the feature cannot be correctly specified.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradictions are clear and unambiguous — BR-1 and BR-2 are directly violated by the feature as stated.","The 1:1 Carrier-to-Shipment invariant and Supervisor-only reassignment are well-established production rules."],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the product team intends to relax the existing invariants or expects a new Sub-shipment aggregate that preserves them at a different level.","The parent-child Shipment lifecycle: SLAs, billing, tracking, and exception handling for sub-shipments.","Whether the 500 kg threshold accounts for indivisible cargo."],"assumptions":["The existing domain model and its invariants are accurately described and currently enforced in production.","The product team has not yet considered the invariant conflicts and is open to design alternatives."]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The auto-split feature requires assigning multiple Carriers to what is logically one Shipment, directly violating BR-1 (exactly one Carrier per Shipment). Even if sub-shipments are modeled as new entities, the original Shipment's Carrier assignment must be dissolved or reassigned — which violates BR-2 (Supervisor-only reassignment) since auto-split explicitly bypasses Supervisor involvement.","affected_stakeholders":["Supervisors","Carriers","Customers","Engineering"],"options":["Option A: Introduce a Sub-shipment aggregate as a first-class domain concept. Each Sub-shipment independently satisfies BR-1 (one Carrier each). The parent Shipment transitions to a 'split' state and no longer holds a direct Carrier assignment. Supervisor approval is required to initiate the split, preserving BR-2.","Option B: Relax BR-2 for system-initiated actions: allow the auto-split system process to act with Supervisor-equivalent authority for weight-triggered splits only. BR-1 is preserved per sub-shipment.","Option C: Reject the feature as specified. Instead, flag overweight shipments for Supervisor review, who then manually splits and assigns Carriers — preserving both invariants completely."],"recommendation":"Option A. It preserves both invariants at the correct aggregate level by introducing Sub-shipment as a new bounded concept, while requiring Supervisor approval for the split action itself. This is domain-honest: it acknowledges that splitting a Shipment is a reassignment-class operation and should remain under Supervisor authority. The 'auto' part can still detect and propose splits, but a Supervisor confirms."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Auto-split assigns multiple Carriers to a single logical Shipment without introducing a sub-shipment aggregate.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1: A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier."},{"violation":"Auto-split bypasses Supervisor involvement when dissolving or reassigning the original Carrier assignment.","business_rule_broken":"BR-2: Only a Supervisor can reassign a Carrier on a Shipment."}]}

Until the product team clarifies whether the invariants should be relaxed, restructured, or preserved via a new aggregate design, the feature cannot be correctly specified.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradictions are clear and unambiguous — BR-1 and BR-2 are directly violated by the feature as stated.","The 1:1 Carrier-to-Shipment invariant and Supervisor-only reassignment are well-established production rules."],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the product team intends to relax the existing invariants or expects a new Sub-shipment aggregate that preserves them at a different level.","The parent-child Shipment lifecycle: SLAs, billing, tracking, and exception handling for sub-shipments.","Whether the 500 kg threshold accounts for indivisible cargo."],"assumptions":["The existing domain model and its invariants are accurately described and currently enforced in production.","The product team has not yet considered the invariant conflicts and is open to design alternatives."]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The auto-split feature requires assigning multiple Carriers to what is logically one Shipment, directly violating BR-1 (exactly one Carrier per Shipment). Even if sub-shipments are modeled as new entities, the original Shipment's Carrier assignment must be dissolved or reassigned — which violates BR-2 (Supervisor-only reassignment) since auto-split explicitly bypasses Supervisor involvement.","affected_stakeholders":["Supervisors","Carriers","Customers","Engineering"],"options":["Option A: Introduce a Sub-shipment aggregate as a first-class domain concept.
